corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: Our work fits into this broad approach, where we take ad- vantage of neural networks to augment document representations prior to indexing; term-based re- trieval then happens exactly as before.

context_window.current: Our work fits into this broad approach, where we take ad- vantage of neural networks to augment document representations prior to indexing; term-based re- trieval then happens exactly as before.

top_candidates.sentence: Architecture and training details of our transformer model are described in Appendix A.

To train and evaluate the models, we use the following two datasets: MS MARCO re-ranking dataset with 8.8M passages 2 obtained from the top-10 results retrieved by the Bing search engine (from 1M queries).
TREC-CAR is a dataset where the input query is the concatenation of a Wikipedia article title with the title of one of its sections.
The released dataset has five predefined folds, and we use the first four as a training set (approx.
If we expand MS MARCO documents using only new words and retrieve the development set queries with BM25, we obtain an MRR@10 of 18.8 (as opposed to 18.4 when indexing with original documents).
We achieve a higher MRR@10 of 21.5 when documents are expanded with both types of words, showing that they are complementary.
We find that the Recall@1000 of the MS MARCO development set increased from 85.3 (BM25) to 89.3 (BM25 + Doc2query).
Results show that BERT is indeed able to identify these correct answers from the improved candidate pool and bring them to the top of the ranked list, thus improving the overall MRR.
As a contrastive condition, we find that query expansion with RM3 hurts in both datasets, whether applied to the unexpanded corpus (BM25 + RM3) or the expanded version (BM25 + Doc2query + RM3).

This is a somewhat surprising result because query expansion usually improves effectiveness in document retrieval, but this can likely be explained by the fact that both MS MARCO and CAR are precision oriented.
90 ms) but is approximately seven times faster than a neural re-ranker that has a three points higher MRR@10 (Single Duet v2, which is presented as a baseline in MS MARCO by the organizers).
The corpus consists of all English Wikipedia paragraphs except the abstracts.
Expanding with copied words gives an MRR@10 of 19.7.

Following this observation, we propose a simple method that predicts which queries will be issued for a given document and then expands it with those predictions with a vanilla sequence-to-sequence model, trained using datasets consisting of pairs of query and relevant documents.
By combining our method with a highly-effective re-ranking component, we achieve the state of the art in two retrieval tasks.
Continuous vector space representations and neural networks, however, no longer depend on discrete onehot representations, and thus offer an exciting new approach to tackling this challenge.

Despite the potential of neural models to match documents at the semantic level for improved ranking, most scalable search engines use exact Expansion is applied to all documents in the corpus, which are then indexed and searched as before. term match between queries and documents to perform initial retrieval.
On the recent MS MARCO dataset , our approach is competitive with the best results on the official leaderboard, and we report the best-known results on TREC CAR .

The corpus needs to be re-indexed every time the expansion technique changes (typically, a costly process); in contrast, manipulations to query representations can happen at retrieval time (and hence are much faster).

The success of document expansion has also been mixed; for example, explore both query expansion and document expansion in the same framework and conclude that the former is consistently more effective.
Given a dataset of (query, relevant document) pairs, we use a sequence-to-sequence transformer model that takes as an input the document terms and produces a query.
Once the model is trained, we predict 10 queries using top-k random sampling and append them to each document in the corpus.
We show in Table examples of queries produced by our Doc2query model trained on MS MARCO.
Nevertheless, the model also produces words not present in the input document (e.g., weather, relationship), which can be characterized as expansion by synonyms and other related terms.
We present the first successful use of document expansion based on neural networks.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
